title: HEEEM: A Hybrid Ensemble Effort Estimation Model for Agile and DevOps Development
authors: Sadaquat Ali Ruk, Yasir Mahmood, Azri Azmi, Nurulhuda Firdaus Mohd Azmi, Salah Bouktif, Sehar Gul
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Accurate effort estimation in Agile and DevOps software projects remains a persistent challenge, as traditional models fail to account for the iterative, continuous delivery nature of modern development environments. Existing single-technique estimation approaches, whether algorithmic, expert-based, or machine learning-based, each capture only a partial view of project complexity, leading to systematic underestimation in Agile DevOps contexts. This study proposes the Hybrid Ensemble Effort Estimation Model (HEEEM), which integrates Use Case Points (UCP) as an algorithmic anchor, the Delphi method as an expert-based judgment layer, and Extra Trees as a data-driven machine learning component. A two-stage framework is adopted: candidate machine learning models are first benchmarked across eight public datasets, spanning 945 projects, providing external validation of the data-driven component and confirming Extra Trees as the most consistent performer (MAE: 0.288, RMSE: 0.398, R2: 0.815), before being embedded into the hybrid ensemble. Empirical evaluation is conducted through case studies on five industrial Agile DevOps projects using standard performance metrics including Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Magnitude of Relative Error (MMRE) and R2.The findings show that HEEEM significantly improved effort estimation accuracy, achieving an in-sample MAE of 21.60, RMSE of 25.32, MMRE of 0.0053, and an in-sample R2 of 0.9983, representing reductions of 69% in MAE, 71.9% in RMSE, and 72% in MMRE compared to individual baseline methods;under Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation, the corresponding out-of-sample metrics are MAE = 72.88, RMSE = 100.42, MMRE= 0.0192, and R2 = 0.9731, representing 45–52% reductions in MAE compared to the algorithmic and expert-based individual baselines. Statistical validation through the Friedman test confirmed these improvements are statistically significant (p = 0.0136). The proposed model offers actionable guidance for project managers and development teams regarding sprint planning, resource allocation, and risk management, and is particularly suited for continuous delivery environments where estimates require regular updating.

title:
Linear and Precise V
                    <sub>FB</sub>
                    Modulation Via LaFMD-Induced Dipoles in TiN/HfO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    /SiO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Gate Stacks Without EOT and Dielectric Thickness Penalty
authors: Wenjun Zhang, Jing Xu, Sirong Liu, Keyou Tong, Xinyue Zhang, Wenjuan Xiong, Shuai Yang, Binbin Xu, Xuanyi Li, Jianfeng Gao, Xiaolei Wang, Jun Luo
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: In this letter, a novel in-situ La(iPr<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-FMD)<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> (LaFMD) gas-treatment dipole technique is proposed for linear and precise flat-band voltage (V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FB</sub>) modulation in TiN/HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> gate stacks. By introducing LaFMD at different locations within the HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> layer, location-controlled La-dipoles are formed, enabling tunable interfacial dipole strength. For the HK-middle/location-controlled samples, a V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FB</sub> tuning range of 137 mV is achieved over 8 HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-cycle intervals, corresponding to an average modulation slope of ~17 mV per HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> cycle. Meanwhile, the maximum modulation range is effectively limited to 200 mV. Furthermore, this approach does not degrade the interface state density (D<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">it</sub>) or trap/detrap electron density (N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ot</sub>), nor does it increase the physical thickness or equivalent oxide thickness (EOT) of the gate dielectric. This work provides an effective approach for linear V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">FB</sub> modulation, enabling high-performance and low-power nanosheet gate-all-around field-effect transistors (NS GAA-FETs) in advanced technology nodes.

title: Decoupled Model Predictive Control for Three-Level Flying Capacitor Boost Converter Based on a Nonlinear Extended State Observer
authors: Cong Wang, Yazhou Wang, Yan Niu, Minghui Yao, Qiliang Wu
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a continuous-set decoupled model predictive control (DMPC) strategy for voltage balancing problem of three-level flying capacitor boost converter (TL-FCBC). To mitigate system coupling effects, a reduced-order state-space equation (ROSSE) of the TL-FCBC is first established by regulating the flying capacitor voltage through an outer-loop controller. This ROSSE introduces an adjustable flexible parameter that can be finely adjusted according to different converter operating conditions. This outer-loop controller adopts the super-twisting sliding mode control (STSMC) to obtain the appropriate duty cycle difference between two switches, thus achieving flying capacitor voltage balance. The proposed DMPC serves as the inner-loop controller, with ROSSE employed as the predictive model, enabling real-time tracking of the reference values for both output voltage and inductor current. In order to further enhance the system robustness against parameter variations, a nonlinear extended state observer (NESO) is developed to accurately estimate the changes of load resistance and input voltage in real-time. By constructing a Lyapunov function, the asymptotic stability of the TL-FCBC system is proven theoretically. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy, which has good voltage balancing capability and robustness.

title: DTMF-Net: Diagnostics-Guided Temporal Multimodal Fusion Network for Financial Forecasting
authors: Gayathri Ramasamy, M Gurupriya, Pritam Gupta
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Financial forecasting in real-world settings requires integrating heterogeneous and asynchronously aligned information sources, including structured market time-series and unstructured financial text. However, existing multimodal approaches often assume perfect temporal alignment or treat all textual inputs as equally reliable, leading to degraded performance under sparse and noisy conditions. This paper proposes a Diagnostics-Guided Temporal Multimodal Fusion Network (DTMF-Net), a causality-preserving framework that explicitly models alignment reliability during multimodal fusion. The proposed method incorporates (i) a market temporal encoder for sequential dynamics, (ii) a text encoder for semantic event representation, (iii) a leakage-aware alignment mechanism that enforces strict temporal causality, and (iv) a diagnostics-guided reliability module that quantifies event coverage, temporal lag, and semantic dispersion to modulate fusion. Experiments conducted on a real-world financial dataset demonstrate that DTMF-Net achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines, with an accuracy of 59.8% and improved performance across AUC, MCC, and regression metrics. Calibration analysis shows improved reliability through lower ECE and Brier score, while uncertainty estimates exhibit meaningful association with prediction error. A leakage validation study shows that relaxed or non-causal alignment can inflate apparent forecasting performance, highlighting the necessity of strict temporal constraints in financial multimodal evaluation. Furthermore, economic backtesting demonstrates improved risk-adjusted returns, with higher Sharpe ratio and reduced drawdown compared to benchmark strategies. These results establish that reliability-aware multimodal fusion, rather than increased model complexity, is critical for robust financial forecasting under asynchronous and low signal-to-noise conditions.

title: Integrating Entropy Regulation and Dual-Objective Optimization for Personalized Exercise Recommendation
authors: Zhonglong Guan, Xinghe Cheng, Zezheng Wu, Ke Liang, Qing Yang, Jiapu Wang, Jingwei Zhang, Shirui Pan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Personalized exercise recommendation systems aim to enhance learning efficiency by dynamically guiding students toward content aligned with their evolving knowledge states. Among various approaches, Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as an effective framework for modeling student-environment interactions as sequential decision-making processes. However, most existing RL-based methods typically reward recommendations that target unmastered knowledge concepts.Such reward-driven strategies are prone to local optima, restricting exploration of unattempted or less familiar knowledge concepts. In addition, they often overlook key learning factors, such as forgetting dynamics and exercise difficulty, leading to suboptimal outcomes. To address these issues, we propose a novel Integrating Entropy Regulation and Dual-objective Optimization Exercise Recommendation (IERDO-ER) method. Specifically, we introduce an entropy-based function to encourage broader exploration in the exercise space. We also design an end-to-end policy network that generates candidate exercises as next-step actions for the recommendation agent. To enable more adaptive and pedagogically sound recommendations, we develop a dual-objective reward mechanism that incorporates both anti-forgetting and gentleness objectives. This mechanism continuously balances policy optimization through a synergy of rewards and penalties. Experiments on three real-world educational datasets show that our approach consistently outperforms strong baselines, validating its effectiveness.

title: A High-SNR SRAM-LUT-Based In-Memory-Computing Macro Supporting Energy-Efficient INT8 and FP8 Computation
authors: Xinhang Zou, Yujin Wang, Luchang Lei, Wenbin Jia, Cheng Peng, Yutong An, Xuanshen Shu, Huazhong Yang, Yongpan Liu, Hongyang Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: Recent quantized neural networks require various dynamic ranges and resolutions to enable better inference accuracy, necessitating mixed support of energy-efficient integer and floating point computation. This work presents an SRAM look-up table (LUT) based INT8/FP8 dual mode in-memory-computing (IMC) macro which supports configurable FP8 (E4M3 and E5M2) multiply-accumulate (MAC) operation and 2:8 structural sparsity pruning. Instead of suffering either excessive overheads of shifting and alignment logic or accuracy degradation for extreme energy efficiency, this macro exploits fine-grain exponent sharing and efficient LUT-based MAC circuits to balance the two aspects. The key features are: (1) a pipelined and SNR-oriented mantissa and exponent execution circuit for FP8 multiplication; (2) a LUT-based shifter and 1-stage adder to reduce digital logic overhead in FP MAC; (3) an in-macro decoder to support 2:8 structural sparsity pruning. The silicon prototype in 28nm CMOS achieves 81.69% accuracy for DeiT-base model for ImageNet dataset in dense FP8 mode, and 76.15% accuracy for ResNet-34 model in CIFAR100 dataset in sparse FP8 mode. The 2:8 pruning provides a 31.42 TFLOPS/W peak energy efficiency and 3.56 TFLOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> peak area efficiency in sparse FP8 mode.

title: Energy Band Engineering Drives Efficient and Stable Self-Powered Ultraviolet Photodetectors
authors: Linyun Zhang, Zexin Yao, Xiaoqing Qiu, Jiancheng Li, Zhongqiang Hu, Hai Zhou
publish: IEEE Electron Device Letters
abstract: Halide perovskites have attracted tremendous attention in optoelectronic devices due to their excellent photophysical properties. However, they are prone to photodegradation under ultraviolet (UV) light, limiting their applications in UV photodetectors (PDs). Here, energy band engineering is adopted to achieve efficient and stable perovskite UVPDs, in which the band gap of the CsPbCl3 microcrystals (MCs) is regulated by Rb doping. The fabricated Cs<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.84</sub>Rb<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.16</sub>PbCl<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> MC film exhibits wider bandgap and improved optical properties. Finally, under 365 nm light, the Cs<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.84</sub>Rb<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0.16</sub>PbCl<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> perovskite PDs show superior UV detection performance including an open-circuit voltage of 0.84 V, an on-off ratio of 2.81×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</sup>, a responsivity of 0.6 A/W and a detectivity of 3.48×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup> Jones. Moreover, the PDs exhibit outstanding stability to UV light and temperature. This work provides a new strategy for the fabrication of high-performance perovskite UVPDs.

title: Decoupling Electrical and Thermal Aging in SiC MOSFETs via Interpretable Frequency-Domain Signatures of Observed Thermal Impedance
authors: Chenyi Wang, Cen Chen, Haodong Wang, Yi Guo, Qisen Sun, Hao Chen, Xuerong Ye, Min Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The coupling of electrical and thermal aging in SiC MOSFETs is a major challenge for accurate online condition monitoring, as decoupling their individual effects remains a primary technical difficulty. This work proposes a method based on the observed thermal impedance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Z_{\text{th,obs}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>), a measurable quantity that contains distinct frequency-domain signatures of both aging mechanisms. Based on this physical principle, a Physics-Informed Feature Distillation framework is presented. The framework uses a knowledge distillation strategy, where a high-capacity Teacher model is first trained on a detailed feature atlas to automatically find the most informative frequency-domain features for aging diagnosis. This knowledge is then transferred to a lightweight Student model, which achieves high accuracy using only this distilled feature set. The proposed methodology is thoroughly tested through simulations and experiments on physically aged devices. Results show that the framework accurately decouples the aging mechanisms, is robust against measurement noise, and is computationally efficient, making it highly suitable for real-time applications. This work provides a practical and interpretable method for online condition monitoring of SiC MOSFETs, paving the way for enhanced operational reliability in SiC power electronic systems through the extraction of decoupled aging indicators.

title: Threaded Dielectric Resonator Antenna With Flexible Assembly and Wideband Frequency Reconfigurability
authors: Hui Xue, Min Tang, Binshan Zhao, Weihong Zhang, Yue-Ping Zhang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: In this letter, a new design of threaded dielectric resonator antenna (TDRA) with thread-engaged dielectric resonator (DR) integration is presented. The assembly strategy facilitates flexible assembly and benefits wideband frequency reconfigurability of the TDRA, while avoiding the alignment problem successfully. Aided by tools such as screwdrivers, the position of the DR within the substrate can be precisely adjusted. The analytical expression for the resonant frequency of the TDRA is provided. Taking advantage of the mechanical tuning scheme, the proposed design achieves a frequency tuning range of 31.6% (3.74 to 5.13 GHz), with radiation efficiency of more than 88%.

title: Collaborative Beamforming with Excitation and Position Diversity for Broadband and High-Resolution Distributed Phased Arrays
authors: Sandra Costanzo, Giovanni Buonanno
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: A distributed phased-array is usually a non-uniformly spaced array with a wireless beamforming, through adequate coordination between the array elements (collaborative beamforming). In this work, a distributed phased-array scheme is proposed for broadband and high-resolution microwave sensing, by combining aspects of probabilistic collaborative beamforming with excitation and position diversity. The absence of grating lobes is understood as a broadband operation, by means of a spatial aperiodicity, whereas high resolution is achieved by considering large spacings between the elements. However, since the aperiodic spacing is probabilistic and the distances between adjacent elements can reach values of several wavelengths, high peak/maximum sidelobe-level may appear. The above diversity is exploited to face the above problem, automatically achieving all benefits coming from the antenna diversity paradigm. Improvements in terms of directivity and signal-to-noise ratio are also discussed, and the possibility of potential multibeam applications is considered. The presence of the element factor is analyzed as well. Typical errors regarding open-loop distributed phased arrays are taken into account.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Unsupervised Domain Adaptation in Spatio-Temporal Echocardiography Segmentation
authors: Arnaud Judge, Nicolas Duchateau, Thierry Judge, Roman A. Sandler, Joseph Z. Sokol, Christian Desrosiers, Olivier Bernard, Pierre-Marc Jodoin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging
abstract: Domain adaptation methods aim to bridge the gap between datasets by enabling knowledge transfer across domains, reducing the need for additional expert annotations. However, many approaches struggle with reliability in the target domain, an issue particularly critical in medical image segmentation, where accuracy and anatomical validity are essential. This challenge is further exacerbated in spatio-temporal data, where the lack of temporal consistency can significantly degrade segmentation quality, and particularly in echocardiography, where the presence of artifacts and noise can further hinder segmentation performance. To address these issues, we present RL4Seg3D, an unsupervised domain adaptation framework for 2D + time echocardiography segmentation. RL4Seg3D integrates novel reward functions and a fusion scheme to enhance key landmark precision in its segmentations while processing full-sized input videos. By leveraging reinforcement learning for image segmentation, our approach improves accuracy, anatomical validity, and temporal consistency while also providing, as a beneficial side effect, a robust uncertainty estimator, which can be used at test time to further enhance segmentation performance. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework on over 30,000 echocardiographic videos, showing that it outperforms standard domain adaptation techniques without the need for any labels on the target domain. Code is available at https://github.com/arnaudjudge/RL4Seg3D.

title: Design and Development of an End-Effector for Fabric Edge Folding Along a Convex Curve With Multiple Pleats_supp1-3687606.mp4
authors: Akira Seino
publish: N/A
abstract: In this article, we propose a novel end-effector designed for folding fabric edges along convex curves. Edge folding is essential in garment manufacturing for fabric parts, such as pockets, front panel hems, and cuffs before sewing. Existing automated edge folding systems rely on templates or fixtures designed for specific fabric parts. These systems must be reconfigured to perform edge folding of different fabric parts. In order to solve this problem, we developed a robot fabric edge folding system along straight lines using the end-effector for free-form folding (F-FOLD) in our previous study. In this article, we propose a new end-effector designed to fold fabric edges along convex curves and straight lines. When the fabric edge is folded along convex curves, generating multiple pleats is necessary to form a smooth folded edge. In this article, we first analyze the pleats generation mechanism and design a new robot end-effector, F-FOLD2, that actively generates pleats during the folding process along convex curves. Experiments show that fold width, rather than curvature, affects the number of pleats, and the average deviation from the desired folding line is within 1.0 mm. These results demonstrate that the proposed F-FOLD2 enables accurate, template-less folding along convex curves and straight lines, offering an alternative solution for flexible, automated garment manufacturing.

title: Joint Robust Estimation of System State and Unknown Input: An Extension of Kitanidis Filter
authors: Bo Tian, Jingting Jia, Wenshuo Li, Xiang Yu
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: As a classical unknown input filtering (UIF) method, the Kitanidis filter is capable of providing the minimum-variance-unbiased state estimation for stochastic systems with unknown inputs. However, it inevitably suffers from the performance degradation in non-Gaussian noise situations due to the limitation of variance criterion. In this letter, a novel UIF algorithm is developed for a class of nonlinear systems subject to non-Gaussian noises and unknown inputs. By constructing a correntropy-metric-based joint optimization problem and introducing a dual-iteration mechanism, the robust estimation of system state and unknown input can be obtained simultaneously, thereby providing enhanced filtering accuracy in non-Gaussian scenarios. Finally, a numerical simulation example is presented to illustrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed algorithm.

title: Neural Scaling Laws for Learning-based Identification of Nonlinear Systems
authors: Marco Roschkowski, Karim Cherifi, Hannes Gernandt
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Control Systems
abstract: The use of machine learning models in system identification has increased due to their ability to approximate complex nonlinear dynamics with high accuracy. However, often it is not clear how the performance of trained models scales with given resources such as data, compute, and model size. A recent development in the machine learning community is the use of so-called neural scaling laws (NSLs) between the amount of training resources and performance of a model. To allow for a better understanding of the scalability of the performance of machine learning models, we empirically verify NSLs in the context of system identification from input-state-output and input-output data using different evaluation metrics for accuracy, different systems and different neural network-based system identification architectures. Our results demonstrate predictable scaling behavior, providing a framework to forecast performance improvements and guide model design and data acquisition strategies.

title: Bridging the Performance Gap: The Impact of Grasping Behavior and Riemannian Geometry on Prosthetic Intent Recognition
authors: Zhongye Chen, Xuefei Chen, Haiheng Wang, Lei Zhang, Suqiu Wang, Shili Liang, Yu Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: The control of myoelectric prostheses is persistently hindered by the poor translation of offline intent recognition accuracy to actual online performance. This study investigates the underlying mechanisms of this offline-online gap from a grasping behavior perspective and proposes a Riemannian geometry-based signal processing scheme. Methods: We hypothesized that the feature distribution mismatch between models dominated by steady-state data and the requirements of online dynamic control is a primary factor in performance degradation. Using datasets from amputees and able-bodied subjects, we quantified the performance disparities of different training models across dynamic and steady-state phases, and evaluated the optimal training data composition for prosthetic control. Finally, based on this optimal data paradigm, we proposed a Riemannian geometry-based signal processing scheme to enhance feature separability. Results: Models neglecting grasping phase variations exhibited significant degradation in the dynamic phase, ranging from 5.85% to 18.16%. Conversely, models trained exclusively on dynamic data demonstrated superior intent recognition. Notably, the proposed method improved dynamic phase performance by up to 13.82%. Conclusion: The mismatch between the steady-state distribution characteristics of training data and online dynamic task requirements is identified as a critical cause of the performance gap. The proposed scheme effectively enhances the model's decoding capability for dynamic signals by optimizing their geometric features in Riemannian space. Significance: This work offers novel behavioral insights into the offline-online gap and provides an effective engineering solution for robust prosthetic systems.

title: Flood-Resilient Substation Hardening with Community-Aware Crew Dispatch Optimization
authors: Amirhossein Ghorbansarvi, Fredrica Arthur, Amin Kargarian, Frederick Weil
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Existing metrics to quantify the impact of power outages on communities are limited. Such metrics are crucial for effective decision-making, particularly during weather events that may result in widespread power outages. This paper proposes a metric for measuring community hardship resulting from power outages and integrates it into a community-aware framework for enhancing grid resilience to flooding. The decision-making problem is formulated as a proactive, look-ahead crew dispatch to install temporary flood barriers (Tiger Dams) around substations. Using preliminary surveys and socio-economic data for East Baton Rouge, we identify income, education level, and home ownership as key indicators of outage-related hardship and aggregate them into zone-level weighting factors via statistical normalization. These weights are embedded in a stochastic mixed-integer optimization model that dispatches crews based on flood-depth scenarios, substation fragility, damage percentage, repair time, and expected energy not supplied (EENS). Numerical results from a 17 -substation synthetic case study demonstrate that the proposed formulation shifts protection priorities toward vulnerable communities while still selecting technically critical substations with large loads and high failure probabilities.

title: Enhancing Equity in Electricity Markets with a ThéVenin-Gpt Framework for Loss Allocation and Tariff Design
authors: Adebayo Adeyinka V, Pelumi Peter Aluko-Olokun, Azeez O. Olasoji
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Unbalanced distribution networks pose challenges to fairness and transparency in loss allocation and tariff design within modern electricity markets. Traditional methods based on proportionality or marginal impacts often underestimate participants' true effects on the system, creating inequities and obscured cross-subsidies. This paper presents a ThéveninGeneral Power Theory (GPT) framework that combines bus-level Thévenin equivalents with GPT decompositions to accurately attribute transmission losses in unbalanced conditions. The approach accounts for active, reactive, and distortion components of power flows, supporting cost-reflective tariffs that match payments with actual contributions. Case studies on IEEE 13-bus and 37-bus feeders show that the new method achieves better allocation accuracy (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>95 \%$</tex>) and fairness indices (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{FI} \approx 0.92$</tex> -0.94) than proportional and marginal methods, while remaining computationally efficient. This framework provides regulators and system operators with a scalable, practical tool for fair tariff design in increasingly complex networks rich in distributed energy resources.

title: Comparative Analysis of Voltage Sag Impacts on Transformer-Less PMSG-Based WECS in Grid Integration
authors: Akinola Ajayi-Obe, Azeez O. Olasoji, Adebayo Adeyinka V, Awodele Kehinde O., Pelumi Peter Aluko-Olokun
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper compares voltage sag impacts on conventional and transformer-less wind energy conversion systems (WECS) based on permanent magnet synchronous generators (PMSG). A major challenge in offshore WECS deployment is the cost and weight of step-up transformers. The study evaluates the behaviour of PMSG-based WECS with and without step-up transformers during symmetrical and asymmetrical grid faults. MATLAB/Simulink models were used to simulate voltage sags, and the resulting effects on DC-link voltage overshoots, current surges, and real power oscillations were analysed. Results reveal that transformer-less configurations exhibit higher DC-link overshoots and require advanced fault ride-through (FRT) strategies. This work provides insight into designing robust FRT techniques for future offshore and high-power wind applications.

title: Undergraduate Research in Iterative Power Flow Using Powermodels and Powerworld Simulator
authors: Nathan Philipello, Jonathan M. Snodgrass, Thomas J. Overbye
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper introduces an iterative, hybrid framework for solving the AC Optimal Power Flow (OPF) problem by integrating PowerWorld Simulator and PowerModels within a unified Python-driven workflow. The approach establishes a closed-loop optimization pipeline that uses PowerWorld for system modeling, exports solved power flow states to MATPOWER format, solves a nonlinear AC-OPF using PowerModels in Julia, and updates generator setpoints back into PowerWorld for the next iteration. Results demonstrate strong consistency between the simulation and optimization environments, validating the bidirectional data exchange and offering a practical template for hybrid OPF studies in complex grid environments.

title: Review of the Major Power Theories for Reference Signal Generation in Switching Compensation of Induction Motors During Voltage Unbalance
authors: Deepak Lama, Prashanna D. Bhattarai
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Voltage unbalance (VU) has become increasingly prevalent in modern distribution networks due to uneven phase loading, unequal feeder impedances, single-phase distributed energy resources, and the widespread adoption of single-phase electric vehicle charging. Induction-motors (IMs), which constitute the majority of industrial loads worldwide, are particularly vulnerable to VU. Even low levels of VU degrade IM performance by increasing losses, reducing torque capability, and accelerating thermal stress. Additionally, during VU, IMs draw stator current that is significantly more unbalanced than the voltage, which in-turn compounds on the existing VU by causing unequal drop along the three phases of system impedance. These impacts intensify the need for effective load balancing and reactive power compensation of IMs. However, unbalanced conditions significantly complicate the design of both passive and dynamic compensators. Some of the recent studies show that capacitor-based passive methods yield inconsistent sizing results under VU and cannot compensate negative-sequence active currents, underscoring the necessity of switching-based compensators. In such devices, accurate reference-signal generation is critical and directly influenced by the underlying power theory. This paper reviews the major power theories used for reference-signal generation in switching compensators, namely the Instantaneous Reactive Power Theory, Current's Physical Components Theory, Conservative Power Theory, and Fryze-Buchholz-Depenbrock Theory, examining their theoretical foundations, implementation requirements, and performance under VU. The review aims to provide a technical foundation for future work on fully compensating IM reactive power under unbalanced conditions.

title: A Framework for Evaluating EV Charging Effects on Power Grids
authors: Mohammadreza Iranpour, Mohammad Rasoul Narimani, Xudong Jia
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: In this study, we present a unified framework for analyzing the impact of electric vehicle (EV) chargers on large-scale power distribution networks. As EV adoption increases, the additional charging load can place significant stress on distribution systems, underscoring the need for tools that assess these impacts and support proactive infrastructure planning. To address this need, we propose a flexible and scalable toolbox that enables high-fidelity analysis using realistic, high-resolution data. The framework consists of three integrated layers: a data layer, an analysis layer, and a visualization layer. The data layer, built in Julia, processes large volumes of EV charging datasets efficiently. The analysis layer uses OpenDSSDirect.jl, a Julia interface for OpenDSS, to perform fast power flow simulations and evaluate grid constraints. The visualization layer leverages QGIS to create GIS-based maps showing spatial variations in network performance based on simulation results. This framework is essential for identifying overloaded or congested lines, locating vulnerable components, and determining optimal sites for future EV charging infrastructure. By combining detailed data processing, scalable grid analysis, and spatial visualization, this work contributes a practical and data-driven toolset for guiding EV integration while supporting grid stability and resilience.

title: Examining Networked Microgrids Resilience Under Heat Wave Using Effective Coordination of DERs
authors: Marco A. Martinez, Paras Mandal
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: Power distribution systems are increasingly vulnerable to extreme weather events, with heat waves posing a significant threat due to increased demand and potential line failures. To address this challenge, this paper presents a simulation-based analysis of resilience enhancement strategies utilizing the IEEE 33-bus distribution system under a realistic heat wave scenario, focusing on the strategic placement of battery energy storage systems (BESS) in coordination with rooftop photovoltaic (PV) generation to improve grid resilience under extreme weather conditions. This involves temperature-dependent modeling of PV along with BESS control strategy to examine the impact of thermally-induced line faults on system performance. The impact of each BESS placement is assessed and compared using techno-economical resilience metrics by analyzing four different case studies within the networked microgrids (MGs). Test results demonstrate a significant enhancement in grid resilience especially in the context of grid voltage stabilization, balancing load demand and supply, and reduction in system cost.

title: Comparison of Performance for Floating Photovoltaic (FPV) and Ground-Mounted PV Systems
authors: Ibrahim Gunes, Robert S. Balog
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive comparative analysis between Floating Photovoltaic (FPV) systems and Ground-Mounted Photovoltaic (GMPV) systems, focusing on performance, environmental, and economic dimensions. Each subsection includes literature insights, recent innovations, and case study discussions. Recent studies have shown significant differences between FPV and GMPV systems in terms of efficiency, cooling effects, and land use. FPV systems offer natural water-based cooling that enhances energy yield, while ground-mounted systems benefit from easier installation and maintenance. However, site constraints, mooring design, and water body characteristics play crucial roles in FPV performance. From an environmental standpoint, FPV reduces land competition and mitigates water evaporation, yet may affect aquatic ecosystems if poorly designed. Economically, the cost of floats, anchoring, and maintenance can influence overall Leveled Cost of Energy (LCOE), but in many cases, FPV installations show competitive or superior returns compared to traditional systems.

title: PSO-Driven Optimization of OLTC Tap Settings in Distribution Systems with Distributed Energy Resources
authors: Byungchan Yoo, Seungmin Jung
publish: 2026 IEEE Texas Power and Energy Conference (TPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an optimization-based approach for determining the effective operating point of an on-load tap changer (OLTC) in distribution networks with high penetration of distributed energy resources (DERs). In conventional systems, OLTCs utilize a line drop compensation (LDC) scheme in which the line impedance is a key parameter for generating the reference voltage. However, the increased reverse power flows caused by DER integration often lead to unintended or frequent tap operations. To mitigate this issue, the LDC impedance parameters are optimized using a particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm so that the OLTC can maintain stable operation under variable renewable generation. The proposed method is evaluated through a distribution system simulation, and the results confirm that the optimized parameters effectively reduce unnecessary tap actions compared with conventional settings.

title: Research on dynamic accounting of carbon emissions in low-carbon mining areas based on multi-source data fusion
authors: Fu Zhaohui, Zhang Ning, Zhao Chunyang, Liu Jincun, Suo Ting, Zhang Jing
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent Manufacturing, Robotics and Automation (IMRA)
abstract: In the context of achieving “dual carbon” goals, precise and real-time carbon emission accounting is urgently needed for the low-carbon transformation of the mining industry. Addressing issues such as insufficient monitoring coverage, single and lagging data sources, and static accounting results in traditional mining area carbon emission accounting, a dynamic accounting method for low-carbon mining area carbon emissions based on multi-source data fusion is proposed. This method systematically integrates multi-source heterogeneous data streams, including automatic energy consumption monitoring data from mining areas, operational data of major energy-consuming equipment, Global Positioning System (GPS) and load information of transportation vehicles, production statistical reports, and remote sensing monitoring data. By constructing a data cleaning, matching, and fusion model under a unified spatiotemporal framework, it achieves refined and high-frequency dynamic tracking and accounting of carbon emission activities throughout the entire coal production chain in mining areas. It can provide scientific basis and dynamic decision support for mining areas to formulate precise emission reduction strategies and evaluate the effectiveness of low-carbon measures. This work provides a novel and scalable technical framework for real-time environmental monitoring and management in the fields of industrial internet of things (IoT) and smart mining.

title: COBIT-NIST Synergy: A Proposed Architecture for Integrated IT Governance, Risk, and Compliance
authors: Feraliana Audia Utami, Lukman Hakim
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Technology, Informatics, Management, Engineering and Environment (TIME-E)
abstract: Effective Information Technology (IT) Governance, Risk Management, and Compliance (IT GRC) is crucial for organizational success, yet achieving a holistic view remains challenging. Construction companies, particularly in dynamic environments like Indonesia, face unique IT risks related to distributed operations, project complexity, and increasing regulatory demands. Existing frameworks like COBIT 2019, focusing on governance and management objectives, and the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF), emphasizing cybersecurity risk management, offer significant strengths but often operate in silos. This paper proposes a synergistic IT GRC architecture that integrates COBIT 2019 and the NIST CSF to provide a more comprehensive and unified approach. The architecture development involved analyzing both frameworks and identifying key integration points. Its applicability and potential benefits were explored through an illustrative case study conducted at a construction company in Indonesia. The proposed architecture facilitates a clearer mapping between governance processes and security controls, potentially enhancing risk visibility and streamlining compliance efforts. The case study provides initial insights into the practical relevance of the integrated approach within the construction sector context

title: A 92.5 TOPS/W Fully-Analog Multi-Layer Computing-in-Memory for State-Space Models in 28nm CMOS
authors: Laith A. Shamieh, Wei-Chun Wang, Narasimha Kidambi, Saibal Mukhopadhyay
publish: 2026 IEEE Custom Integrated Circuits Conference (CICC)
abstract: An SRAM-based Fully Analog STate-space Compute-in-Memory (FAST-CIM) is demonstrated for State Space Models (SSMs). The all-analog SSM includes a recurrent-state-update CIM cascaded with feedforward CIMs, maintaining signals in the analog domain across macros with a single digitization point. A 28 nm CMOS test-chip demonstrates compute efficiency up to 92.5 TOPS/W and compute density of 28.8 TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>.

title: Fog Computing for Intelligent and Sustainable IoT: Architectures, Optimization, Datasets, and Open Challenges
authors: Prachi Chaturvedi, Shahnawaz Ahmad, Arvind Mewada
publish: 2026 IEEE Madhya Pradesh Section Conference (MPCON)
abstract: The limitations of centralized cloud computing in IoT applications, which require low latency, high bandwidth, and mobility, have been demonstrated through the growth of the IoT ecosystem. Fog computing eliminates these limitations by distributing computing, storage, and networking closer to data sources across the edge-cloud continuum. This survey summarizes recent work on fog computing in terms of architectural design, resource management and scheduling; AI-based optimization; security/privacy issues; and energy efficiency. The survey also shows that fog systems can help reduce latency and bandwidth for real-time analytics in intelligent cities, healthcare, vehicle-to-vehicle networks, and industrial IoT.

title: Performance Monitoring of a Dual Axis Sensor Based Solar Tracking System using IoT
authors: Sakina Atoui, Said Boudjana, Yousra Ouail, Loubna Kherrour
publish: 2025 First International Conference on Mechanical and Electrical Engineering for Green Energy Technologies (MEEGET)
abstract: This study investigates enhancing performance monitoring of a dual axis sensor based solar tracking system STS using internet of things IoT. An IoT connected model is used to reduce energy consumption, maintain costs, and utilize energies more efficiently. By using the user interface, the user can initiate himself to solve any problems due to environmental conditions. Physical devices have sensors that send data to a centralized platform. The energy management system is used to control, monitor and optimize the performance of the production system. The monitored data are electrical parameters including current, voltage and supplied power. Real-time IoT monitoring is implemented and tested.

title: Effect of variable blade pitch on the aeroacoustic performance of a Darrieus rotor
authors: Guermache Ahmed Chafik, Talamalek Ayoub, Khalfallah Smail
publish: 2025 First International Conference on Mechanical and Electrical Engineering for Green Energy Technologies (MEEGET)
abstract: Small-scale vertical-axis wind turbines are designed for installation in urban areas or on building rooftops. However, people in the vicinity of these turbines may experience noise pollution caused by the rotor during operation. The noise emissions are primarily associated with the interaction between the rotor blades and the turbulent airflow, referred to as aerodynamic noise, which includes airfoil self-noise and blade tip noise. Additional noise sources originate from mechanical components, such as bearings, vibrations, supporting arms, and the rotating shaft. This mechanical noise can be reduced through effective mechanical engineering design. In contrast, aerodynamic noise can be mitigated using flow control methods or shape optimization processes. The present paper aims to evaluate the aeroacoustic performance of a dynamic blade-pitched Darrieus vertical-axis wind turbine using numerical simulations. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) is employed to calculate the rotor's aerodynamic characteristics, while the acoustic levels are determined using the Ffowcs Williams and Hawking equations. These two disciplines are fully coupled within the commercial software ANSYS Fluent, meaning the acoustic field is computed based on the fluid properties during the simulations. The two-dimensional CFD model is validated using available published experimental data on the power coefficient, while the computational aeroacoustic (CAA) model is validated through comparison with a three-dimensional case. The appropriate source correlation length (SCL) is determined, and it is found that this parameter correlates well with the blade span. Study findings indicate that blade pitch amplitude affects power coefficients and acoustic emissions. For power generation, ± 25° is recommended as pitch amplitude, which does not influence the acoustic emission. Results also show that as tip speed ratios increase, more noise is generated. Dynamic blade-pitched Darrieus rotor emits a lower amount of wake noise than fixed-blade Darrieus rotors, which is justified by the highest vortex emission in the case of fixed blade pitch. Generally, the present paper contributes to understanding the impact of variable blade pitch on power extraction and noise reduction.

title: Multi-Sensor Fusion-Based Composite Map Construction
authors: Gang Bai, Shuo Kai, Wenli Li, Yongfei Xiao, Yongguo Zhao, Hao Wu, Zhengguang Ma
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To address issues such as insufficient mapping accuracy, limited semantic representation, and weak navigation adaptability of mobile robots in enclosed complex environments like tunnels, this paper proposes a composite map construction scheme integrating multi-sensor data with semantic grids. As critical infrastructure in the transportation, mining, and water conservan cy sectors, tunnels present challenges in inspection and maintenance operations due to high environmental risk factors and substantial labor costs, urgently requiring mobile robots to replace manual labor for autonomous tasks. For the safe and efficient operation of mobile robots in tunnels, accurate identification of targets such as personnel, equipment, and obstacles is indispensable to support obstacle avoidance, path planning, and task decision-making. First, a multi-sensor fusion framework is established. Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) is employed to fuse data from the Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), laser odometer, and wheel odometer. Factor graph optimization is integrated to eliminate cumulative errors, achieving high-precision pose estimation. Second, the YOLOv13 object detection algorithm is introduced to extract environmental semantic information. Object categories, locations, and confidence levels are mapped onto the grid map constructed by the LiDAR, generating a composite map that integrates both geometric structure and semantic features to enhance environmental description capabilities.

title: LUIST-DETR: A Lightweight Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Infrared Small Target Detection Algorithm Based on RT-DETR
authors: Yongcheng Wang, Chaofeng Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: This paper presents LUIST-DETR, a lightweight variant of the RT-DETR model tailored for infrared small target detection on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). To accommodate the computational limitations of edge deployment, we integrate PRConv modules into the backbone for efficient feature extraction, simultaneously employ an optimized multi-scale neck network SSFF, and adopt the Focal-Wise-MPDIoU loss function to improve bounding box regression under challenging infrared conditions. Evaluated on the HIT-UAV dataset, LUIST-DETR achieves 82.5% mAP50 and 53.6% mAP50:95, with only 13.7 million parameters and 44.0 GFLOPs, surpassing baseline models such as RT-DETR-R18 and YOLO variants in both accuracy and computational efficiency while maintaining real-time performance. Ablation studies and comparative analyses further validate the complementary effects of the proposed modules, which effectively reduce missed detections and false positives—making the model well-suited for applications in surveillance and traffic monitoring.

title: Path Planning for Autonomous Vehicles Based on an Improved RRT* Algorithm
authors: Lidong Qi, Weidong Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To address global path planning for autonomous vehicles in complex environments, an improved RRT* algorithm based on heuristic sampling is proposed. A bidirectional search is first employed to quickly obtain a feasible initial path, which is pruned using a greedy strategy to remove redundant points and determine its length for constructing an initial elliptical sampling region. The improved RRT* performs sampling within this region, incorporating goal-biased sampling and artificial potential field guidance to enhance convergence. Parent reselection and rewiring are conducted within a dynamic circular neighborhood to further optimize the path. Finally, redundant points are removed. Simulation results in MATLAB under various scenarios, evaluated using a normalized weighted composite metric, demonstrate that the proposed method generates efficient global paths effectively and rapidly.

title: Adaptive Participant Grouping to Enhance Personalized and Privacy-Aware Federated Learning in the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)
authors: Nagarjun Thanneeru, Manasa Thotla, Navadeep Santosh Pampana, Manoj Reddy Adla, Jashwanth Reddy Vireddy
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: Federated Learning (FL) allows training privacy-applications on a large number of distributed nodes, but it suffers as there is no IID approach, unreliable federated training, and ineffective personalization in healthcare IoMT devices. A framework on adaptive participant grouping is proposed and combines the FedProx regularization, the use of trust in the weighted averaging among participants and the process of differential privacy and MAML based personalization into a multimodal federated structure. Federated members are dynamically formed into three classes of high-, medium-, and low-trust according to the consistency of updates and the stability of validation in which low-trust nodes can consist of noisy nodes. The results of the framework were tested on five rounds of communication with multimodal medical data, with the results showing that the framework attained an accuracy score range from 89 % to 96 % when the model is federated trained. The adaptive grouping method increased client trust reliability because it delivered 21 percent better trust score stability than standard federated aggregation methods. Findings indicate better strengthen, adaptation reliability control, and privacy protection in distributed healthcare (systems).

title: Machine Learning–Based Geospatial Analysis for Disaster-Resilient Renewable Energy Systems
authors: D. Ganesh, M. Sunil Kumar, R.Sujith Reddy, P. Sangeetha, R.Satish Kumar, P. Dileep
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: Nowadays, the rising cases of natural disasters such as hurricanes, earthquakes, floods, etc. have led to severe destruction in relation to human life, infrastructure, and the economy. In traditional practices of analyzing disasters, the techniques have mainly relied upon past events. In this consideration, the study aims to analyze the past data of disasters to determine the behaviour of disasters in association with their geographical distribution over time. Machine learning approaches are used to improve the analysis in the study, whereby the Long Short-Term Memory model is utilized to analyze the dynamics of disasters over time, and the DBSCAN algorithm is used to analyze the geographical areas prone to disasters. By integrating the analysis of data with the visualization of geospatial analysis, the solution promotes how historical disaster data can be used to provide insightful information. The solution highlights the potential of combining machine learning analysis with geospatial analysis to enhance preparedness and identification related to disaster risk management.

title: Machine Learning-Based Toxicity Prediction of Industrial Chemicals Using Tox21 Database: Toward Applications in Epichlorohydrin, Acrylonitrile, and Styrene-Based Polymers
authors: Weiming Duan, Qi Zhang, Yinzi Shao, Fangwei Xue
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: This study develops a machine learning pipeline for predicting compound toxicity using the Tox21 database, targeting applications in industrial polymer chemistry. The pipeline integrates Fisher score-based feature selection, SMOTE oversampling, cross-validated grid search, and post-hoc threshold tuning to address severe class imbalance. Four classifiers-improved Random Forest, SVM-RBF, SVM-Linear, and AdaBoost-are compared across all 12 Tox 21 endpoints. The improved Random Forest achieved the highest AUC of 0.903 on the primary NR.AhR target, and threshold optimization improved sensitivity by up to 230 percent. Multi-target evaluation yielded mean AUC exceeding 0.74 for all classifiers. The pipeline provides a foundation for toxicity screening of epichlorohydrin, acrylonitrile, styrene, and their polymeric derivatives.

title: Fallguard-Det: an Efficient Fall Detection Algorithm Based on Deep Learning
authors: Feng Lin
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: Fall detection is a critical yet challenging task for the safety of the elderly. To address the limitations of manual monitoring and wearable sensors, this paper presents FallGuardDet, an efficient fall detection framework based on YOLOv12. Our approach introduces three key innovations to handle drastic posture variations and complex background interference: (1) a Multi-Scale Human Posture Perception (MSHPP) module to fuse cross-level features for diverse scale perception; (2) a Dynamic Posture-Aware Attention Mechanism (DPAM) to adaptively focus on fall-related regions and mitigate occlusions; and (3) a Key Posture Feature Enhancement (KPFE) module to refine human contour representation and reduce misidentifications. Experimental results show that FallGuard-Det outperforms mainstream object detection methods in both accuracy and inference speed, demonstrating its superior effectiveness for realtime practical applications.

title: A Fault Diagnosis System for Aerospace Bearings Based on LSTM-RNN and Envelope Spectrum Analysis
authors: Zhiyuan Gu, Ziwei Zheng, Yunlong Wu, Shouzhao Sheng
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Advanced Sensing, Intelligent Manufacturing (ASIM)
abstract: Aviation rolling bearings are critical components in aerospace machinery, and their operational integrity directly influences system performance and safety. Due to harsh operating conditions, these bearings are susceptible to wear and failure, necessitating timely and accurate fault diagnosis. This paper proposes a fault diagnosis and classification system based on Long Short-Term Memory Recurrent Neural Networks (LSTM-RNN) combined with envelope spectrum analysis. A dataset comprising 10 fault types with a sampling frequency of 12 kHz is used. Envelope spectra are constructed to extract corresponding amplitude and statistical features. These features are used to generate envelope-based sample sequences, which are divided into training, validation, and test sets. Both single-label and multi-label classification strategies are implemented using LSTM-RNN architectures. Multi-class performance is assessed using the Kappa coefficient, and classification results are visualized via t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE). The classification accuracy and effectiveness under different sample sizes are further analyzed through accuracy-sample size curves. The proposed method demonstrates high classification accuracy and robustness, offering a promising solution for intelligent fault diagnosis in aerospace bearing applications.

title: An Optimal Control Model for Vehicle Path Planning and Motion Control Based on TD3 Algorithm with Multi-Dimensional Penalty Mechanism
authors: Ruotao Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Advanced Algorithms and Control Engineering (ICAACE)
abstract: To address the optimization problem of vehicle path planning and motion control under complex terrain conditions, this paper proposes an optimal control model integrating a multidimensional penalty mechanism with the TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) algorithm. Based on the vehicle's state parameters in the Global Coordinate System (GCS) and Body Coordinate System (BCS), the model constructs a multiobjective reward function system including distance penalty, energy consumption penalty, collision penalty, wheel angle penalty, action smoothness penalty, and motor power constraint. By clarifying the mapping relationship between vehicle state quantities (coordinates, attitude angles, velocities, etc.) and action quantities (acceleration, steering angle), combined with the impact of terrain on vehicle speed, a vehicle motion equation is established. Meanwhile, to meet the requirements of path planning efficiency, safety, and control stability, a hierarchical penalty mechanism is designed: single-step and global distance penalties guide the path toward the target; energy consumption penalties related to rolling friction, aerodynamic drag, and gravity work achieve energysaving optimization; collision penalty ensures driving safety; wheel angle and action smoothness penalties reduce control mutations; and motor power constraint ensures the rationality of power output. The optimal strategy is solved based on the TD3 algorithm, and the planning results and control effects are intuitively presented through visualization methods. Verification shows that the model can efficiently generate vehicle motion strategies that balance optimal path, minimum energy consumption, and stable control under complex terrain and multiconstraint conditions, providing a reliable solution for vehicle autonomous navigation and intelligent control.

title: Data Fusion Methodology for a Hyperspectral-LiDAR System
authors: Marco Bignardi, Nikos Tsoulias, Burawich Pamornnak, Dimitrios S. Paraforos
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Agriculture and Forestry (MetroAgriFor)
abstract: The demand for sustainable farming and climate change are driving the use of advanced monitoring techniques in the industry. Precision Agriculture (PA) meets this need by using sensor-based systems to optimize resource efficiency and crop yields. Fruit crops such as grapevines require detailed monitoring of plant morphology and physiology, necessitating high-resolution data. LiDAR scanning provides accurate 3D reconstruction for carbon and biomass estimation, while hyperspectral imaging offers spectral data related to plant physiology and biochemistry. However, no sensor is infallible when used in isolation. This article therefore suggests using a fusion system combining hyperspectral imaging and LiDAR to generate spectral 3D point clouds. Geometrical calibration using a chessboard pattern was used to synchronize the sensors so that spectral data can be projected onto 3D geometry. The system was tested in laboratory setting, demonstrating the possibility to fuse the data from the two devices to produce a "spectral point cloud".

title: Precision 3D Mapping of Forests and Crops Using a Visual-LiDAR System Mounted on a Rover
authors: Laura Chilin, Sebastiano Chiodini, Federico Toson, Carlo Bettanini, Adam Młynarczyk, Marcello Coradini, Carmelo Maucieri, Francesco Pirotti, Giacomo Colombatti
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Agriculture and Forestry (MetroAgriFor)
abstract: Autonomous exploration and mapping technologies are becoming increasingly important tools for environmental monitoring, especially in forested and semi-structured natural environments. Within the European E-FORESTER project, a modular and adaptable visual–LiDAR system has been developed to support ecological data collection through robotic platforms. The SILVA rover served as experimental platform for testing perception, navigation, and 3D reconstruction capabilities in unstructured terrain. The results demonstrate the feasibility of using multimodal sensing for terrain understanding, environmental monitoring.

title: AGROPATROL-A Multi-Functional Terrain Roaming Bot for Field Surveillance
authors: Sivakami S, Inbarasu A, Magesh V, Mithun Srinivasan J
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Trends in Material Science and Inventive Materials (ICTMIM)
abstract: Agro Patrol is an intelligent multi-functional terrain-roaming robot developed to address critical agricultural challenges such as labour shortages, the increasing cost of operation, inefficient use of pesticides, and the absence of real-time monitoring of the crops. The system combines soil moisture sensors, pesticide detector modules, GPS positioning, and high-resolution imaging to enable continuous and dynamic tracking of the field. Unlike conventional static sensing systems and drone-based monitoring, Agro Patrol performs close-range inspection and physical intervention through terrain-adaptive mobility and intelligent obstacle avoidance. An AI-driven sensor fusion framework processes real-time environmental, soil, and visual data to accurately identify crop stress, pest infestation, and irrigation requirements under varying field conditions. The precision spraying mechanism ensures targeted chemical application, significantly reducing pesticide wastage, soil contamination, and environmental impact. The communication pipeline made possible by IoT can be remotely monitored and supported in real-time to enhance irrigation planning and crop protection even when the farmland is large. Agro Patrol can provide a next-generation, efficient, and sustainable solution to precision agriculture with better productivity, crop quality, and long-run profits for farms with enhanced energy control, modular architecture, and scalable design.

title: Mobile Multi-Camera System for 3D Fruit Tree Scanning and Apple Size Estimation
authors: Jordi Gené-Mola, Jochen Hemming, Eduard Gregorio
publish: 2025 IEEE International Workshop on Metrology for Agriculture and Forestry (MetroAgriFor)
abstract: Three-dimensional modeling of fruit trees is necessary for implementing precision agriculture strategies in orchard management. Among the available technologies, photogrammetry-based techniques have proven effective for reconstructing accurate dense point clouds using high-resolution handheld cameras, but their manual image acquisition limits scalability in commercial settings. To overcome this limitation, we present a low-cost mobile multi-camera system to automate image acquisition for orchard 3D reconstruction. The multi-camera (MultiCam) system was built using eight synchronized RGB cameras and evaluated against a previously validated handheld photogrammetric methodology. Both systems were used to scan apple trees in an experimental orchard, and their resulting point clouds were compared in terms of geometric quality and performance in fruit size estimation. Reconstruction was performed using Structure-from-Motion and Multi-View Stereo pipelines. Apple diameters were estimated using four methods, with the least squares sphere fitting approach providing the most accurate and robust results. Despite the lower resolution of the MultiCam images, the system achieved comparable accuracy in fruit diameter estimation to the handheld approach, with a mean absolute error of 5.1 mm (vs. 4.5 mm) and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.91 (vs. 0.93). The proposed solution significantly reduces acquisition time and simplifies the scanning process, offering a practical and scalable alternative for in-field fruit monitoring using photogrammetry.

title: A cylindrical electrical and ultrasonic multi-imaging system based on piezoelectric composites
authors: Akira Kimoto, Yui Adachi
publish: 2025 18th International Conference on Sensing Technology (ICST)
abstract: In this paper, an ultrasonic and electrical multiimaging system which consists of sixteen piezoelectric composites is developed. This system makes it possible to reconstruct both ultrasonic and electrical images of the living body since the piezoelectric composite has low acoustic impedance as one of the characteristics. Ultrasonic waves are measured by piezoelectric composites which are directly contacted to the living body, and the electrical conductance is measured by using the surface electrodes of piezoelectric composites. From their measured values, ultrasonic and electrical images are respectively reconstructed. In addition, the information of the living body is clearly detected since the electrical imaging is improved by using the ultrasonic image. In the experiment, the ultrasonic and electrical images at the living body model which plaster was inserted in $0.1 \% \mathrm{NaCl}$ solution tank were demonstrated by the developed multi-imaging system. The system was evaluated.

title: From Files to URLs: A Unified ML-Based Vulnerability Scanner
authors: Bayan Abu Shawar, Muath Alshaikh, Sajnin Sultana, Jasim Majid, Suhib Melhem, Majdi Sawalha
publish: 2025 26th International Arab Conference on Information Technology (ACIT)
abstract: This paper introduces a machine learning-based vulnerability scanner that identifies malware and malicious URLs through on-demand scanning, utilizing trained models instead of static signatures. This system is also based on two significant modules: analysis of Portable Executable (PE) files based on the properties of file headers and analysis of URLs based on structural analysis. The scanner is scaled and can be extended in the future, e.g., with real-time monitoring, because it is integrated through a web interface with user login, scan history, and visualization of the result. Evaluation with publicly available data shows reasonable quality detection rates compared with traditional methods, and offers a high-quality and scalable solution to high-stakes cybersecurity challenges.
